question: What first drew you to Business Management, and how has that interest developed since?
lookingFor: A genuine, evolving story grounded in a specific experience or observation — not 'I like people and money', but a moment that sparked real intellectual curiosity about how organisations work.

bullets: Name a specific trigger — a family business, a news story, a school project, a book, or an event you observed
Show how curiosity developed: reading, work experience, a club or enterprise activity
Land on a specific aspect of business that now genuinely interests you intellectually
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My uncle runs a small logistics company, and I spent a summer helping in the office the year it nearly went under — watching him navigate cash flow, staff morale, and a supplier dispute simultaneously was genuinely gripping. I started reading around it: first Michael Lewis's The Undoing Project for behavioural economics, then Peter Drucker's The Effective Executive. What really caught me was realising that management isn't just common sense dressed up; there's a rigorous discipline of trade-offs behind every decision. Since then I've run the school's enterprise club and pushed it from seven members to forty, which taught me more about motivation and delegation than any textbook.

commonMistakes: 'I want to run my own business one day' with no intellectual substance behind it
Motivation based purely on salary or prestige
A story that hasn't visibly evolved — no evidence of reading or active engagement since the initial spark
followUps: Which area within Business Management — strategy, finance, operations, people — interests you most right now, and why?

question: Why do you want to study Business Management at this university specifically?
lookingFor: Specific, researched reasons tied to this department — evidence of genuine curiosity about what makes this course distinctive, not generic praise or ranking-worship.

bullets: Name something specific: a module, a live consultancy project, an accreditation, a research centre, or a faculty member's work
Connect it to a concrete personal interest or experience
Show you understand how this course differs from what similar institutions offer
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Your course is one of the few at undergraduate level that embeds a real consulting project in the second year — working with an actual SME rather than a case study. That distinction matters to me because I learn by doing, and I've seen in my enterprise club how different real constraints are from hypothetical ones. I also read Professor Osei's work on stakeholder management in family businesses, which connects directly to what I observed in my uncle's company. I didn't want a generic business degree; I wanted one structured around applying theory under genuine pressure, and yours is built that way.

commonMistakes: Praising the university's general reputation or league-table position with nothing underneath
Describing modules that belong to a different institution's course
Motivation that would apply identically to any business school
followUps: What would you bring to the cohort that other applicants might not?

question: Tell us about a business book, article, podcast, or case study you've engaged with recently that isn't part of your school syllabus.
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual curiosity beyond the classroom — the ability to engage critically with an idea rather than simply summarise it.

bullets: Name a specific source and what it argues — not just the title
Engage critically: what did you agree with, push back on, or want to explore further?
Connect it to a broader question in business or management you now think about differently
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been working through Clayton Christensen's The Innovator's Dilemma — the central argument is that good management can actually cause a company to fail by over-serving its best customers and ignoring newcomers at the low end. What struck me is that this isn't a story about incompetence; it's about rational decisions creating irrational outcomes at scale. I found myself questioning it when I looked at companies like Netflix, which disrupted Blockbuster but is now itself being disrupted by short-form platforms. It made me wonder whether disruption is a perpetual cycle rather than a one-time event, and I'd love to explore that properly in an economics or strategy module.

commonMistakes: Naming a book but only summarising it — no personal critical engagement
Choosing something very well-known (Rich Dad Poor Dad) without being able to go beneath the surface
Failing to connect the source to a wider business question
followUps: What's the strongest counterargument to the book's main claim?

question: What do you understand by 'competitive advantage', and can you give an example of a company that has built one sustainably?
lookingFor: Conceptual grasp of competitive advantage — the idea that it comes from doing something differently or better in a way that is hard to copy — paired with a real, analysed example.

bullets: Define competitive advantage without jargon: something that lets a firm outperform rivals sustainably
Identify the source — cost leadership, differentiation, network effects, brand, or unique resources
Analyse the example rather than just naming it: explain why the advantage persists
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Competitive advantage is what lets a company earn returns its competitors can't easily replicate — it has to be durable, not just a temporary edge. IKEA is the example I find most interesting: its advantage isn't just low prices, it's a whole interlocking system — flat-pack logistics, in-store showrooms that double as warehouses, designs that optimise material use, and customer self-assembly that transfers cost. Any competitor trying to copy one element finds the others missing. What makes it genuinely sustainable is that the whole system is the advantage, which makes it extremely hard to disassemble and replicate.

commonMistakes: Defining competitive advantage as simply 'being better than competitors' without explaining the mechanism
Naming Apple or Amazon with nothing more than 'they're innovative'
Confusing competitive advantage with current market share
followUps: How might that advantage erode over time, and what would the company need to do about it?

question: Should a business's primary obligation be to its shareholders or to a broader set of stakeholders? What's your view?
lookingFor: A structured, reasoned position — not a rehearsed answer, but evidence the applicant has thought about the tension between shareholder primacy and stakeholder capitalism and can defend a view.

bullets: Acknowledge the genuine tension: shareholder primacy has a coherent theoretical basis; so does stakeholder theory
Bring in a real-world reference — Milton Friedman vs. the Business Roundtable 2019 statement, or a specific company example
Commit to a view while conceding what the other side gets right
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the shareholder-first model made sense in a world where capital was the scarce input and externalities were easy to ignore, but neither of those is true now. Friedman's argument that a manager's sole duty is profit has a clean logic, but it assumes markets price in environmental and social costs — which they demonstrably don't. The 2019 Business Roundtable statement was interesting because it was the first time major CEOs publicly acknowledged that long-run value and stakeholder health are intertwined, not opposed. My view is that a company ignoring its employees, suppliers, or community is not serving shareholders well in the long run — it's just externalising costs onto the future. But I'd also push back on vague 'purpose' language that becomes a shield for accountability.

commonMistakes: Sitting on the fence without committing to any position
Presenting only one side with no engagement with the counterargument
Vague answers about 'balance' that show no real knowledge of the debate
followUps: Can you name a company that you think gets this balance right — and one that gets it wrong?

question: What is the difference between a flat and a hierarchical organisational structure, and what are the trade-offs of each?
lookingFor: A clear conceptual distinction paired with genuine understanding of the practical trade-offs — not a dictionary definition but evidence the applicant can think about when each structure fits.

bullets: Define both structures clearly in plain language
Name one real advantage and one real drawback for each
Show awareness that the best structure depends on the company's size, industry, and strategy
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A hierarchical structure has multiple layers of management with a clear chain of command; a flat structure has few layers and wide spans of control, meaning more people report directly to senior managers. Hierarchies give clear accountability and work well in large organisations where coordination is complex — a hospital or airline can't run on consensus. But they can slow decisions and create silos. Flat structures are faster and often better for innovation because information travels directly, which is why many tech startups use them. The problem is that as a flat company grows, the absence of structure becomes a burden — managers get overwhelmed, and informal power replaces formal, which can be less transparent. I find it interesting that the 'right' structure is really about what the company needs at its current stage.

commonMistakes: Describing one as simply 'better' than the other
Missing the span-of-control concept entirely
No examples or context to ground the definitions
followUps: Can you think of a company that changed its structure as it grew, and what drove that change?

question: What do you understand by market segmentation, and why does it matter to a business?
lookingFor: A clear conceptual grasp of segmentation — dividing a market into groups with shared characteristics — and genuine understanding of why it improves targeting and resource allocation.

bullets: Define segmentation clearly: dividing potential customers into groups based on shared needs or characteristics
Explain why it matters: not every customer is the same, and undifferentiated marketing wastes resources
Give a concrete example of a company that segments effectively
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Market segmentation is dividing a large potential market into subgroups whose members share similar needs, behaviours, or characteristics — geographic, demographic, psychographic, or behavioural. It matters because treating all customers identically almost guarantees you serve none of them optimally. Nike is a good example: it markets running shoes very differently to competitive athletes versus casual gym-goers, using different channels, pricing, and messaging. Without segmentation, it would either underserve the serious runner or alienate the casual buyer. The discipline is in identifying which segments are big enough and accessible enough to be worth targeting, which is a genuinely hard strategic question.

commonMistakes: Defining segmentation as simply 'targeting different customers' without explaining the mechanism
Confusing segmentation with positioning
No example to ground the concept
followUps: How would you segment the market for an electric toothbrush startup entering the UK?

question: What is the difference between profit and cash flow, and why can a profitable business still fail?
lookingFor: Clear understanding of the profit vs. cash flow distinction — one of the most practically important concepts in business — with a credible explanation of why solvency can collapse despite profitability.

bullets: Define profit (revenue minus costs over a period) and cash flow (actual money in and out) without conflating them
Explain the timing mismatch: profit is accrual-based; cash flow is real-time
Give a realistic scenario — for example, a company selling on credit with long payment terms while paying suppliers upfront
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Profit is what a business has left after all costs are deducted from revenue over a given period — it's an accounting measure. Cash flow is the actual movement of money in and out of the business in real time. They diverge because of timing: a company can record a sale as revenue the moment a contract is signed, but the customer might pay ninety days later, while staff, rent, and suppliers need paying now. A fast-growing business is especially vulnerable — it can be booking record profits while running out of cash to pay its bills, because growth consumes working capital. That's why 'profitable but insolvent' is a real phenomenon, not a contradiction. Understanding that distinction is what made me realise finance isn't just about the bottom line.

commonMistakes: Treating profit and cash flow as the same thing or near-equivalents
A vague answer about 'expenses exceeding income' that conflates both concepts
No credible mechanism for why a profitable business runs out of cash
followUps: How would you monitor cash flow risk in a small business you were advising?

question: What do you think motivates people at work beyond pay, and does this match any theory you've come across?
lookingFor: Genuine curiosity about human motivation in organisations — the ability to connect personal observation or experience to a management theory without just reciting it.

bullets: Identify non-financial motivators drawn from real observation or experience
Name and briefly apply a relevant theory — Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor, or similar
Show critical awareness: don't treat the theory as gospel
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: From running our school enterprise club, I noticed that the members who stayed longest weren't the ones offered the most responsibility — they were the ones who felt their ideas were actually heard. That connects to Herzberg's two-factor theory: he argues that pay and basic conditions are hygiene factors — their absence demotivates, but their presence doesn't actively motivate. The real drivers are things like achievement, recognition, and meaningful work. What I find compelling is that Herzberg was writing in the 1950s and yet you still see it borne out today — companies that pay well but have poor culture struggle to retain people. I'd want to test whether the theory holds equally across different cultures and industries, because I suspect it doesn't.

commonMistakes: Reciting Maslow's hierarchy without any critical commentary or personal connection
Claiming money is the only motivator
Listing theories with no sense of which one you find most convincing or why
followUps: Do you think what motivates people changes as they move up in an organisation?

question: Tell us about a business story you've followed recently and what you found most interesting or surprising about it.
lookingFor: Evidence that the applicant reads business news habitually and can analyse it — not just report it. The panel wants to see genuine curiosity and the ability to identify what's interesting and why.

bullets: Name a specific recent story — not a years-old case study
Go beyond what happened: why does it matter, what does it reveal about a broader business dynamic?
Show a personal analytical angle, not just a neutral summary
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been following the collapse of several challenger banks that grew aggressively on cheap customer acquisition but never built a profitable revenue model — they had millions of users and no path to profit. What surprised me most was that investors funded them for years because growth was treated as a proxy for value, even without underlying unit economics. It's a live example of something I'd read about in the context of the dotcom bubble: the moment sentiment shifts and capital gets expensive, the model unravels. The interesting question to me is whether the regulators who approved their banking licences should have stress-tested their business models more rigorously. I don't think the answer is straightforward, but I'd want to study it properly.

commonMistakes: Describing a story that is several years old as if it's recent
Summarising the headline with no analysis of what it reveals
Choosing a story with no genuine personal angle — just a report
followUps: What would you have done differently if you'd been advising the board of that company?

question: Is it ever ethical for a company to do something legal but harmful — for example, targeting vulnerable consumers with high-interest products?
lookingFor: Structured ethical reasoning that moves beyond 'if it's legal, it's fine' — the ability to engage seriously with the gap between legality and morality in a commercial context.

bullets: Acknowledge the tension: legality and morality are not the same threshold
Engage with a real-world example or sector — payday lending, gambling, fast food, tobacco
Take a defensible position and show you've weighed the counterargument
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Legality sets the floor, not the ceiling. A company can pass every regulatory test and still behave in ways that are ethically problematic — payday lending to people in financial distress is a good example: technically lawful, commercially rational, and genuinely harmful in aggregate. I think the interesting test is whether the company is creating genuine value for the customer or primarily extracting value from their vulnerability or lack of information. If a product's profitability depends on customers not understanding what they're buying, something has gone wrong regardless of the small print. My view is that ethical companies should hold themselves to a higher standard than the law requires, partly because it's right and partly because the long-run reputational and regulatory costs of not doing so are real. But I'd also push back on the idea that every business facing a vulnerable customer is exploiting them — the question is intent and design.

commonMistakes: Concluding that anything legal is automatically ethical
Condemning all such businesses without engaging with genuine complexity
Avoiding a position entirely — the panel wants to see you commit
followUps: Where would you draw the line, and how would you operationalise that as a manager?

question: What are the main challenges a UK company might face when expanding into a new overseas market?
lookingFor: A structured understanding of international market entry barriers — legal, cultural, competitive, financial — not a generic list but a prioritised, reasoned account.

bullets: Identify at least three distinct challenge categories: regulatory, cultural, competitive, and financial/currency
Explain why each matters — not just list it
Show awareness that the challenges vary by market and industry
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: There are several layers, and the weighting depends heavily on where you're going. Regulatory and legal compliance is usually the first hurdle — understanding local employment law, tax, product standards, and data protection varies enormously between markets. Cultural fit is subtler but arguably more costly when it goes wrong: a brand positioning that works in the UK may not translate, and assuming it will is a mistake many companies make. Competitive dynamics are different too — there's often a local incumbent with deep distribution and customer trust that a newcomer underestimates. And currency risk is real: if you're pricing in pounds but your costs or revenues are in a volatile currency, your margins can evaporate without anything operationally going wrong. I think the most underrated one is cultural intelligence — it's hard to measure and easy to underinvest in.

commonMistakes: Listing challenges without explaining why each one matters
Treating all international markets as equivalent
Forgetting currency and financial risks entirely
followUps: Which entry mode — exporting, franchising, joint venture, direct investment — would you recommend for a UK food brand entering Japan?

question: What distinguishes an entrepreneur from someone who simply starts a business?
lookingFor: A thoughtful distinction that goes beyond superficiality — ideally touching on risk tolerance, innovation, and the drive to change something, not just the act of trading.

bullets: Distinguish entrepreneurship from self-employment or business ownership in a meaningful way
Bring in the concept of innovation and value creation — not just exchange
Ground it with an example or a real contrast
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd say the distinction is about innovation and intentional risk — an entrepreneur is trying to change something, not just trade. Someone who opens a hairdressing salon is taking on commercial risk and is to be admired for it, but they're replicating an established model. An entrepreneur is trying to create something that doesn't yet exist or does something fundamentally differently. Schumpeter's phrase 'creative destruction' captures it well — real entrepreneurship disrupts existing markets, which is why it's uncomfortable. By that definition, plenty of people inside large organisations are entrepreneurial (sometimes called intrapreneurs) without owning any equity. What I find interesting is that the trait seems to correlate less with risk-seeking and more with an intolerance for the status quo.

commonMistakes: Defining an entrepreneur simply as 'someone who starts a business'
Conflating entrepreneurship with self-employment
No reference to innovation or value creation
followUps: Can an entrepreneur exist inside a large corporation? What enables or prevents that?

question: The COVID-19 pandemic exposed significant weaknesses in global supply chains. What do you think companies learned from that, and what would you do differently as a supply chain manager?
lookingFor: Understanding of supply chain vulnerability — specifically the trade-off between efficiency and resilience — and the ability to reason practically about what better design looks like.

bullets: Identify the core vulnerability: just-in-time models optimised for efficiency, not disruption
Explain the trade-off: lean supply chains are cheaper but fragile
Propose realistic responses: nearshoring, supplier diversification, buffer inventory, dual sourcing
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The pandemic showed how fragile just-in-time supply chains are when a global shock hits. Companies had stripped out inventory and single-sourced from the cheapest supplier — often in one geography — because it was efficient. That worked brilliantly in normal times and catastrophically in a disruption. What smart companies are now doing is rethinking the resilience-efficiency trade-off: holding slightly more inventory as a buffer, diversifying supplier geography, and mapping their supply chains two or three tiers deep rather than just to their direct suppliers. If I were managing it, I'd want to run regular stress tests — what happens if our main port closes, or our primary supplier fails? Resilience has a cost, but the pandemic showed that the cost of ignoring it is far higher.

commonMistakes: Describing the problem without engaging with what should be done differently
Treating 'just-in-time' as inherently bad rather than a rational trade-off
No concrete operational measures — only vague calls for 'diversification'
followUps: How would you weigh the cost of extra resilience against the efficiency loss when advising a board?

question: How has digital technology changed the relationship between businesses and their customers in the last decade?
lookingFor: A structured, analytically grounded answer — not a vague 'the internet changed everything' response. The panel wants to see awareness of data, direct relationships, power shifts, and new expectations.

bullets: Identify at least two concrete, distinct changes: data and personalisation, disintermediation, customer power, speed of feedback
Show awareness of both opportunity and risk for businesses
Ground it with a real example
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Three things stand out to me. First, the direct relationship — digitally native companies can now reach customers without intermediaries like retailers or distributors, which shifts both margin and data to the brand. Second, personalisation at scale: a company like Spotify recommends content to 600 million people as if it knows each of them individually, which was inconceivable a decade ago. Third, the speed and visibility of customer feedback: a bad product experience now propagates in hours via social media, which means quality and trust are non-negotiable in a way they weren't before. The risk is that companies have more data than they know how to use well, and there's a genuine question about privacy and consent that's going to define how this evolves. I think the relationship has fundamentally shifted from transactional to something more like ongoing negotiation.

commonMistakes: Answering at the level of 'social media changed marketing' without going deeper
Ignoring the data and privacy dimension entirely
No specific company or sector example to ground the analysis
followUps: Which industries do you think have managed this shift best, and which have struggled — and why?

question: What is corporate social responsibility, and do you think it's genuinely changing how businesses behave or is it mostly a marketing exercise?
lookingFor: A clear definition of CSR plus an honest, reasoned view on whether it drives real change — the panel wants intellectual honesty, not an uncritical celebration of CSR.

bullets: Define CSR clearly: the idea that businesses have obligations beyond profit to employees, communities, and the environment
Acknowledge both genuine examples and the risk of 'greenwashing'
Take a position — partial scepticism is fine if it's argued
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: CSR is the idea that a business has responsibilities beyond generating profit — to its employees, the communities it operates in, and the environment. In principle I think it's important; in practice I'm partly sceptical. There are genuine examples: Patagonia structuring its entire supply chain around environmental accountability and donating profits to conservation is not a marketing exercise. But there's also a lot of 'greenwashing' — companies making headline pledges with weak underlying commitments. My view is that CSR is most credible when it's embedded in the business model and carries a real cost, and least credible when it's a PR layer on top of unchanged practices. The interesting shift I see is that employees and investors are now demanding substance, which creates structural pressure that PR alone won't satisfy.

commonMistakes: Treating CSR as unambiguously positive with no critical awareness of greenwashing
Dismissing it entirely as pure marketing with no acknowledgement of genuine examples
Defining CSR too narrowly as only charitable giving
followUps: How would you design a CSR programme for a company that you thought was actually serious about it?

question: Tell us about any work experience, volunteering, or enterprise activity you've done. What did you learn about how organisations actually work?
lookingFor: Genuine reflective insight drawn from real experience — not a description of what you did, but what you now understand differently about business or management as a result.

bullets: Briefly describe the experience — enough context for the panel
Focus on what surprised you or what you now see differently
Connect the insight to something you want to explore further in the degree
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent two weeks doing work experience at a small accountancy firm, which I chose partly because I wanted to see numbers in a real context. What surprised me most was how much time the partners spent on relationships — with clients, with regulators, with each other — and how little of that shows up in any description of what an accountant does. The technical work was important but largely delegated; the senior people spent their energy on judgement calls and trust-building. It made me realise that management is largely about navigating people and information, not just processes. I want to understand that dynamic properly, which is why the organisational behaviour modules in this course caught my attention.

commonMistakes: Describing tasks completed rather than what was learned
Choosing an experience with no connection to the business world
Insight that's generic — 'I learned communication is important' — with no specificity
followUps: Was there anything about how the organisation was run that you would have done differently?

question: A company has to decide whether to launch a new product with incomplete market data. How should the leadership team approach that decision?
lookingFor: Structured reasoning about decision-making under uncertainty — not paralysis and not recklessness, but a framework for acting with incomplete information.

bullets: Acknowledge that waiting for perfect information is not usually an option — there's a cost to delay too
Describe practical approaches: minimum viable product, staged rollout, sensitivity analysis, risk-adjusted scenarios
Show awareness of who should be involved and what cognitive biases to guard against
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Perfect information is never available, and waiting for it indefinitely has its own cost — a competitor might move first, or the window might close. I'd want the leadership team to start by being clear about what they actually don't know and what they could find out cheaply: a small-scale pilot or MVP can replace some of that missing data with real signal rather than modelling. Then I'd want a structured look at the downside: what's the worst-case scenario, is it survivable, and what would trigger an exit? Scenario analysis rather than a single forecast is more honest about uncertainty. The other thing I'd flag is cognitive bias — if the product is a pet project of the CEO, confirmation bias can quietly shape the 'analysis'. Building in a formal devil's advocate role or a pre-mortem exercise is worth the friction.

commonMistakes: Recommending they simply 'gather more data' without engaging with the cost of delay
Treating it as a purely financial decision with no discussion of decision process quality
No mention of cognitive bias or governance
followUps: Who in the organisation should have the final say on that decision, and how would you structure the governance around it?

question: What do you think are the biggest people-management challenges facing businesses today, and why?
lookingFor: Awareness of contemporary HR themes — hybrid work, skills gaps, diversity, retention, wellbeing — with genuine analytical depth rather than a list of buzzwords.

bullets: Identify two or three specific, current challenges rather than a generic list
Explain the mechanism: why is this challenge acute right now?
Show a view on which challenge is most structurally important
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Three things strike me as genuinely significant. The skills gap is probably the most structural: the pace of technological change means roles are evolving faster than organisations can train people or hire for. That puts enormous pressure on learning and development, which most companies underinvest in because returns are hard to measure. Second, the post-pandemic renegotiation of what people expect from work — more flexibility, clearer purpose, faster feedback — has raised the bar for what 'a good employer' means, and companies that haven't adapted are losing people. Third, and perhaps most underrated, is psychological safety: Amy Edmondson's research shows it's one of the biggest predictors of team performance, but building it requires managers who are trained and incentivised to create it, which is harder than it sounds. I think the skills question is the most urgent because it compounds over time.

commonMistakes: Listing buzzwords (diversity, wellbeing, hybrid) without explaining why they're challenges now
Treating people management as secondary to strategy or finance
No prioritisation — the panel is listening for your judgement
followUps: How would you measure whether a people-management initiative was actually working?

question: Tell us about a well-known business failure that you've studied or read about. What went wrong and what can other businesses learn from it?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine independent research into business history, paired with analytical depth — a real diagnosis of why the failure happened, not just 'they made bad decisions'.

bullets: Name a specific, researched failure — not a vague reference
Diagnose the root cause: was it strategy, culture, governance, financial engineering, or external shock?
Draw a transferable lesson — what should other businesses do differently?
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've read a lot about the collapse of Kodak, which I find fascinating precisely because they didn't fail through ignorance — they actually invented the digital camera in 1975 and shelved it. The failure was cultural and structural: a business model built on high-margin film meant that digital was internally seen as a threat rather than an opportunity, and the incentives rewarded protecting the existing revenue stream. By the time the market moved, they'd lost a decade. The lesson isn't 'embrace digital' — it's that the organisational culture and incentive structure will consistently override formal strategy if they point in different directions. A company can have a visionary strategy document and a culture that quietly kills it. I think that dynamic explains far more corporate failures than external shocks do.

commonMistakes: Choosing a very recent failure with insufficient distance to analyse properly
Describing what happened without diagnosing why
A lesson that is so generic it explains nothing — 'they should have planned better'
followUps: Are there parallels between that failure and anything happening in business today?

question: Why do some companies charge a premium price for products that cost little more to produce than cheaper alternatives?
lookingFor: Understanding of value-based pricing, brand equity, and perceived value — the idea that price reflects what the customer believes something is worth, not just what it cost to make.

bullets: Distinguish between cost-based and value-based pricing clearly
Explain the role of brand, signalling, and perceived quality
Give a specific example where the premium is credibly justified
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Premium pricing works when the customer's willingness to pay exceeds the cost of production by a large margin — and that gap is created by perceived value, not actual cost. A Louis Vuitton bag and a high-street equivalent may use similar materials, but consumers aren't buying leather; they're buying a signal about identity, taste, and exclusivity. That's brand equity: the premium the brand itself commands independently of the physical product. What I find interesting is that the premium can be self-reinforcing — charging more can actually increase perceived quality because of price-quality signalling, which is why discounting a luxury brand can permanently damage it. The discipline is in deciding which market you're serving: a premium price strategy only works if you've genuinely built the brand equity to support it, otherwise customers feel cheated.

commonMistakes: Assuming premium pricing is simply about higher production costs
Ignoring the psychological and signalling aspects of price
No example to anchor the explanation
followUps: Could a premium pricing strategy ever backfire — and under what conditions?

question: What is break-even analysis, and why would a startup founder find it useful before launching?
lookingFor: A clear, practical grasp of what break-even analysis is and what question it answers — not just the formula, but why it matters for decision-making under uncertainty.

bullets: Define break-even: the output level at which total revenue equals total costs
Explain the distinction between fixed and variable costs
Explain why it's specifically useful pre-launch: it makes assumptions explicit and tests viability
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Break-even analysis tells you how many units you need to sell — or how much revenue you need to generate — before you start making a profit. It works by separating your costs into fixed costs, which stay the same regardless of output (rent, salaries, equipment), and variable costs, which change with every unit produced. The break-even point is where total revenue covers both. For a startup founder, I think it's valuable before launch precisely because it forces assumptions into the open: you can't do the analysis without committing to a selling price, an estimate of variable costs, and a view on fixed overheads. If the break-even point requires selling ten thousand units a month but your market research suggests five hundred, that's a signal worth acting on before you've spent the money.

commonMistakes: Defining break-even as simply 'not making a loss' with no reference to fixed and variable costs
Reciting the formula without explaining why it's useful
No connection to what a founder would actually do with the analysis
followUps: What are the limitations of break-even analysis as a planning tool?

question: The majority of mergers and acquisitions fail to create the value promised. Why do you think that is?
lookingFor: A structured, reasoned account of why M&A so often disappoints — covering strategic overestimation, cultural integration failure, the winner's curse, and principal-agent problems — not just 'they paid too much'.

bullets: Identify the most common root causes: overpayment, overestimated synergies, cultural clash, integration failure
Go beyond the obvious: explain the principal-agent dynamic (executives who benefit from deal size) and the winner's curse
Take a view on which cause is most underappreciated
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: There are several layers to why M&A underperforms. The first is straightforward: acquirers typically overpay in competitive auctions because of the winner's curse — the firm that wins is often the one that was most optimistic about synergies. Second, synergies get systematically overestimated in the pitch deck and underdelivered in execution — it turns out that combining two sales teams or two IT systems is far harder and more expensive than projected. But the cause I find most interesting is cultural integration: two organisations with different values, management styles, and informal norms are being asked to behave as one, often while key talent is heading for the exit. That's a people problem, not a finance problem, and it's often treated as an afterthought. There's also a principal-agent angle: deals are often driven by CEOs whose pay and prestige are tied to size, not returns to shareholders.

commonMistakes: Answering only 'they paid too much' without going deeper
Ignoring the cultural and human dimension — which is where most value destruction happens
No mention of the governance or incentive problem
followUps: What due diligence would you want to do on the cultural fit before recommending an acquisition?

question: What is a platform business model, and why has it come to dominate so many industries in the last twenty years?
lookingFor: A clear conceptual grasp of platform economics — particularly network effects and the creation of value between user groups — and a genuinely analytical view on why the model has proved so powerful.

bullets: Define a platform business: it creates value by facilitating interactions between two or more user groups
Explain network effects: the platform becomes more valuable as more people use it
Identify why this model wins at scale: low marginal costs plus winner-take-most dynamics
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A platform business doesn't produce a product directly — it creates infrastructure that facilitates value exchanges between two or more groups. Airbnb doesn't own hotels; it matches hosts and guests. The key mechanism is network effects: every new host makes the platform more attractive to guests, and every new guest makes it more attractive to hosts. This is self-reinforcing, which is why once a platform reaches critical mass it's extremely hard to displace. The economic power comes from the fact that the marginal cost of adding another user is near zero — the platform scales without proportional cost increases, which is unlike traditional manufacturing or retail. What I find striking is that the model creates genuine winner-take-most dynamics, which raises real questions about competition and regulation that I'd want to explore in an industrial economics module.

commonMistakes: Confusing 'platform' with any tech company
Missing the network effect mechanism — which is the defining feature
No engagement with why the model dominates now versus twenty years ago
followUps: Is there any industry you think is resistant to platform disruption, and why?

question: How do you think artificial intelligence will change what managers actually do over the next decade?
lookingFor: A nuanced, evidence-grounded view — not AI-panic or AI-utopianism, but a realistic account of which managerial functions AI will change and which will remain distinctively human.

bullets: Distinguish between tasks AI will automate (data processing, pattern recognition, reporting) and what it cannot (judgement, trust-building, ethical navigation)
Engage with specific managerial functions: decision-making, performance management, planning, communication
Show awareness that the management challenge is partly how to use AI well, not just what AI replaces
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think AI will compress the routine analytical work of management significantly — processing performance data, generating forecasts, flagging anomalies, drafting reports. What it won't easily replicate is the human work of management: building trust, exercising judgement in genuinely ambiguous situations, navigating conflict, reading a room. A manager who's good at those things but bad at data will be augmented by AI; a manager who's only good at processing data and producing reports faces a harder question. What I find interesting is that this might shift what organisations value in managers: emotional intelligence, ethical reasoning, and the ability to ask good questions of AI-generated outputs may become more important, not less. The real challenge is that many organisations will not navigate this transition well — they'll either ignore AI or overautomate, and the managers who do best will be those who treat AI as a collaborator, not a replacement or a threat.

commonMistakes: Claiming AI will replace all managers — which ignores the distinctively human elements of the role
Dismissing AI's impact and treating management as unchanged
No engagement with what specific managerial tasks are affected and how
followUps: Would you want to work in an organisation that had heavily automated its management processes? What would be your concern?
